_metadata.description: CLO Quality & Outcome Hierarchy — well-formedness criteria (AoL 6-criterion rubric), outcome hierarchy (ILO→PLO→CLO→LLO), BYUI's 5 kinds of outcomes, behavioral vs cognitive objective formats, observable verb reference, Merrill's performance-content matrix.
_metadata.runtime_strategy_rationale: Body MD ~10,533 chars / 4 ≈ 2,633 tokens — within the 'either' band per runtime_strategy_rules. Set to read_at_runtime per named_override_patterns.selective_load: canvas_course_expert loads 2-3 of ~12 knowledge files per audit task. Selective-load defeats embed-cache benefits.

scope: Framework for evaluating whether learning outcomes are well-formed BEFORE auditing whether they are aligned. Alignment checks that outcomes are wired together; this framework checks that the outcomes themselves are worth wiring. Boundaries: alignment traceability lives in course_design_language Principle 6; Bloom verb classification lives in taxonomy_explorer.

facts.definition: ILO (Institutional Learning Outcome — university-wide graduate goals, indirect/proxy measures). PLO (Program/Major — competencies for a degree, direct + indirect). CLO (Course Learning Outcome — what a student achieves by completing the course, direct measures). LLO (Lesson/Class — what a student achieves in a single session, direct within session). Rule: Classroom outcomes accomplish course outcomes; course outcomes combined accomplish program and institutional outcomes. If any level is broken, the level above is unsupported.
citations: Morrison et al. (2019); BYUI Learning and Teaching

facts.definition: University Academic Outcomes → Foundations Outcomes → College Outcomes → Department Outcomes → Major Outcomes → Course Outcomes → Lesson Outcomes (general → specific).

Knowledge (information, concepts, facts); Character & Values (attitudes, professional behavior, judgment); Skills (observable abilities students can perform); Experiences (meaningful contexts that shape perspective); Learning-to-Learn (metacognitive habits, self-regulation). Most audits find CLOs only in Knowledge and Skills buckets — the high-value gap is almost always Character & Values.

Every outcome: 'The student will be able to: [action verb] + [subject-content reference]'. Optional additions sharpen precision: level of achievement ('with 90% accuracy') and conditions of performance ('Given a client scenario').

Most precise format. Specifies verb + content + optional standard + optional conditions. Best for mastery-based or competency-based programs. Example: 'Given five black-and-white photographs of insects, the learner will correctly identify the three insects detrimental to corn.'

More flexible format. States a general outcome, then lists 2-3 sample behaviors that indicate mastery. Best for higher-order outcomes where the full range of performance can't be specified in advance. Example: 'Interprets a graph. (1) Determines which group sold the most. (2) Identifies groups performing below average. (3) Determines the year with the greatest number of sales.'

BYU-Idaho's Assurance of Learning rubric scores CLOs on six dimensions: Scope (3-6 ideal, 3-8 acceptable), Language Clarity (no jargon/subjective/comparative language), Measurable (observable action verb), Single-barreled (one goal per CLO), Rigor (mix of know/do/become verb levels appropriate to course level), Relevance (logical fit for course), Recency (current industry practice/research). Scope and Measurability failures are most common and most fixable; Rigor failures are most consequential.
citations: BYU-Idaho Assurance of Learning CLO Rubric AoL

facts.definition: Most common writing error: stating what the instructor does or what activity students complete, instead of what the student achieves. Examples: 'To view a videotape on ecological safeguards' (activity, not outcome); 'The instructor will describe types of fire extinguishers' (instructor-focused); 'Students will understand the water cycle' (non-observable verb). Test: 'Is this outcome what I want the learner to know or demonstrate after completing the topic?'

Remembering: arrange, define, duplicate, label, list, match, memorize, name, order, relate, repeat, reproduce, state. Understanding: classify, describe, discuss, explain, express, identify, indicate, locate, report, restate, review, select, sort, tell, translate. Applying: apply, choose, demonstrate, dramatize, employ, illustrate, interpret, operate, practice, prepare, schedule, sketch, solve, use. Analyzing: analyze, appraise, calculate, categorize, compare, contrast, criticize, diagram, differentiate, discriminate, distinguish, examine, experiment, question, test. Evaluating: argue, assess, defend, design, determine, evaluate, judge, predict, rate, value. Creating: combine, construct, decide, estimate, formulate, organize, predict, rank, synthesize, write. Flag verbs (non-observable): understand, know, appreciate, be aware of, learn, feel, recognize.

Classifies each CLO by content type AND performance level. Fact (specific association — recall only). Concept (category/class — recall or application). Principle/Rule (relationship between concepts — recall or application). Procedure (sequence of steps — recall list, or application perform). Interpersonal (spoken/nonverbal interaction — application). Attitude (disposition — application, inferred from behavior). A course designed around Fact-Recall objectives cannot develop Procedure-Application competencies.

Flag: no outcome statements visible in course intro/module overviews/syllabus; outcomes use 'understand/know/appreciate' or other non-observable verbs; <3 or >8 CLOs; all CLOs cluster at same Bloom level (no rigor spread); outcomes describe instructor activities; all outcomes are Knowledge-type (no Character/Values, Skills, Experiences, Learning-to-Learn); rubric criteria don't trace back to a named CLO.
citations: This knowledge file's Canvas Audit Indicators

facts.definition: Value enum: meets_criteria | partial | needs_revision. Companion tag clo_criteria_flags lists which of the 6 AoL criteria fail: scope, clarity, measurable, single_barreled, rigor, relevance, recency.
citations: This knowledge file's Audit tag section

sources: Morrison, G.R., Ross, S.M., Morrison, J.R., & Kalman, H.K. (2019). Designing Effective Instruction, 8th ed. Chapter 5. Wiley.
BYU-Idaho Learning and Teaching. Learning Outcomes. learningandteaching.byui.edu
BYU-Idaho Assurance of Learning. CLO Rubric AoL (internal document).
Anderson, L.W., Krathwohl, D.R., et al. (2001). A Taxonomy for Learning, Teaching, and Assessing.
